text: We would like to present a robotic assisted placement of a hepatic artery infusion pump and catheter for delivery of regional chemotherapy. The patient is a sixty-two year old male who presented with increasing fatigue and weight loss and found to have a near obstructing mass in his ascending colon. A CT scan showed multiple bilobar liver metastases. He underwent resection of his primary tumor and completed six cycles of adjuvant therapy. Repeat imaging showed a moderate response to therapy, but he still had significant bilobar disease. He then underwent a robotic assisted placement of a hepatic artery infusion pump for delivery of FUDR regional chemotherapy.

Six ports are visible: a 12mm peri-umbilical port for the robotic camera, three 8mm robotic working ports, and assistant ports in the right and left lower quadrants. The surgeon uses a liver retractor to expose the area, excises the gastropathic ligament, and begins dissection of the common hepatic artery.

The surgeon uses the robotic system to precisely dissect and excise station eight A hepatic artery lymph nodes, which are sent to pathology. Lymphatics are ligated using the ligature device. The dissection includes the common hepatic artery and gastroduodenal artery, with additional eight A lymph nodes being resected and retrieved.

The surgeon isolates the right gastric artery, clips it, and excises it using a ligature. Subsequently, the gastroduodenal artery (GDA) is dissected free, extending the dissection for at least three centimeters.

The surgeon inserts the hepatic artery infusion catheter into the abdomen. A bulldog clamp is applied proximally on the gastroduodenal artery (GDA), which is then ligated distally using a 2-0 silk tie and a metal clip. An arteriotomy is performed on the GDA, and the catheter is inserted and secured with silk ties. Methylene blue is injected into the catheter to confirm proper distribution to both liver lobes without leakage into the stomach or duodenum.
